data.title: Wintrust Financial: Premium Franchise, But Macro Concerns Keep Upside Capped
data.description:
Thesis

Wintrust Financial (WTFC) is a high-quality player in the regional banking space that has demonstrated superior loan growth, robust deposit growth, and a brilliant multi-charter structure model. However, severe macroeconomic uncertainty related to the Iran war taking interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve off the table, concerns around the private credit market, and overall credit deterioration in the economy make the upside limited. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on WTFC.

Company Profile

Wintrust is a financial services company headquartered in Rosemont, IL, with over $60 billion in assets. The firm operates through 175 community bank locations and offers traditional and commercial community banking, wealth management, mortgage origination, commercial insurance premium financing, and short-term accounts receivable financing. Wintrust operates in the Chicagoland area, southern Wisconsin, and northwest Indiana. Wintrust operates under a unique multi-charter community banking model with 16 separately chartered community banks. Wintrust had $71 billion in total assets, $53 billion in total loans, and about $57 billion in total deposits as of Q4, 2025.

Fourth Quarter, 2025 Earnings

Wintrust reported a strong finish to 2025 with Q4, 2025 earnings that produced an 11% increase in net income year-over-year from $525.1 million in Q4, 2024 to $583.9 million in Q4, 2025. This increase in net income was driven by growth in average earning assets and improved net interest margin. Net Interest Income (NII), the difference between the interest the bank earns on its assets, like loans and investments, versus the interest the bank pays out on its liabilities, like deposits, increased $583.9 million in the fourth quarter. This metric is up $16.9 million from the third quarter, driven by the growth in average earning assets.

The company also reported strong balance sheet growth, with assets increasing $1.5 billion in the fourth quarter, driven by loans and liabilities increasing $1.3 billion, driven largely by organic deposit growth. Finally, on the credit side, the Provision for Credit Loss (PCL), an income statement item that accounts for losses on troubled loans, jumped $16.9 million from $567 million in the third quarter to $583.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2025. Management attributed these "qualitative attributes" added to the provision related to credit spreads and equity market valuations per the Q4, 2025 earnings presentation.

Digging deeper into the rise in the PCL shows that nearly the entire increase was caused by a line item titled "Provision for unfunded lending-related commitments losses - Other," while the amount allocated to actual loan losses (Provision for loan losses - Other) actually dropped from $19.6 million in Q3, 2025, to $14.3 in Q4, 2025. Unfunded lending-related commitments are lines of credit like commercial revolvers and construction loans. These are approved loans that have not yet been drawn on by the borrower.

The significant increase in PCL allocated to this item ties to our macroeconomic risks because it shows management is preparing for stressed middle-market and corporate borrowers that may need to quickly access lines of credit to shore up liquidity due to a deteriorating macroeconomic environment. While this can be seen as a proactive step by management to prepare for potential weakness in the economy, it does warrant our attention. Despite the issues revolving around the PCL, Wintrust's Q4, 2025 earnings demonstrated a strong organization that is well-managed and exploiting a strategic advantage with the community-focused business model.

Return Metrics

The first return metric we will examine, and arguably the most important, Net Interest Income. Wintrust has demonstrated strong growth in net interest income from Q1, 2024, to Q4, 2025, with a growth rate of about 26% over the time period. The growth in NII over this time period is largely attributed to growth in Wintrust's average earning assets, with a $5.5 billion increase in loans. This level of NII growth shows the firm's strategy of acquiring assets is working well and producing strong returns.

Net Interest Margin (NIM) measures NII divided by average earning assets. This metric tells us how much NII the bank is generating from its interest-earning assets like loans and investments. Wintrust has consistently produced NIM in the mid-three percent range, demonstrating the bank is generating healthy NII from its earning assets. For reference, the peer median NIM as of Q4, 2025 was 3.13%. Moreover, Wintrust sits at the top of its peer group in terms of NIM as of Q4, 2025, at 3.52%. This metric further reinforces the strategic capabilities of Wintrust and its management.

The final return metric we will examine is Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE). This metric assesses the net income generated from the bank's tangible common equity, that is, the common equity after removing intangible assets like goodwill, which can arise from non-core activities like acquisitions. This metric gives us valuable insight into how much income the bank is generating from its capital base. Over the prior eight quarters, Wintrust's ROTCE has hovered close to the peer median of 15%. These values for ROTCE show that Wintrust is competitive in terms of using its capital base to generate income for shareholders.

Company Structure

As mentioned earlier, Wintrust operates under a unique multi-charter community banking model. Wintrust operates under 16 separately chartered community banks, giving it local branding, decentralized decision-making, and deep community ties. This differentiates Wintrust from cookie-cutter community banks in that each of the 16 banks has its own local board of directors and its own localized loan committee. This type of local banking creates brand loyalty and "stickier" deposits that may be less rate-sensitive than those of competitors. Perhaps the greatest advantage of Wintrust's structure is their unique MaxSafe deposit program. Since Wintrust has 16 separate bank charters, a client can spread out deposits across the charters and receive 16 times the standard FDIC insurance limits, or $4,000,000. This increased insurance limit gives clients additional peace of mind, particularly after the events of 2023, when we saw deposit runs on major banks.

Balance Sheet and Credit Quality

Turning to the balance sheet, Wintrust reported exceptional loan growth across all major loan categories in 2025, growing loans to $53.11 billion in 2025 from $48.06 in Q4, 2024, representing about a 10% increase. A large portion of this increase came from commercial and commercial real estate loans. Wintrust has consistently delivered solid loan growth going back to 2015, growing at a compound annual growth rate of 12% for the prior 10 years. This level of growth shows the company's business development efforts are sound and provide strong results.

On the deposit side, Wintrust grew deposits to $57.72 billion in Q4, 2025, up from $52.51 billion in Q4, 2024, representing an approximate 9.7% increase. The firm's strategy of acquiring deposits has yielded a 12% compound annual growth rate over the past 10 years, showing that the strategy is working well. Management attributed the deposit growth in 2025 to targeted marketing efforts, a diverse product offering, and gains in market share, partially attributed to the Macatawa acquisition completed in August 2024. This robust growth in deposits shows the firm is able to capture deposits at an excellent rate to further fuel growth.

Moreover, non-interest-bearing deposits are the cheapest source of funding for banks, as they allow the bank to lend out nearly zero-cost funds to generate loans. Wintrust's ratio of non-interest-bearing deposits to total deposits has consistently exceeded 19%, a quality level in the regional banking industry. This ratio of non-interest-bearing deposits further demonstrates Wintrust's sound balance sheet and growth strategies.

On the credit quality side, we will examine two critical metrics for banks: net charge-offs to average loans, which measures the dollar value the bank has written off for bad loans net of recoverables to its average loans, and non-performing loans to total gross loans, which measures the ratio of troubled loans to the bank's total loans.

Net charge-offs to average loans tell us the portion of the bank's loans that are being written off. Over the prior eight quarters, net charge-offs to average loans averaged 0.18%, with a brief spike in Q2, 2024, resulting from "gradual normalization of credit metrics" per management. However, upon further inspection, the jump in net charge-offs in Q2, 2024 can largely be attributed to the commercial real estate portfolio generating a $15.5 million charge-off with only $90,000 in recoveries. While the downward trend from Q2, 2024 is promising, the net charge-offs should garner attention in the future, with about 26% of the loan book being allocated to commercial real estate.

Next, we will examine non-performing loans (NPLs) to total gross loans, which gives us insight into the ratio of Wintrust's troubled loans versus its total loans. Over the prior eight quarters, NPLs to total gross loans have averaged 0.36%, meaning less than 1% of the bank's loan book are considered problem loans. The stability of non-performing loans to total gross loans over time can be attributed

Macroeconomic Environment

Wintrust has demonstrated excellent growth metrics in 2025 and remains a high-quality player in the regional banking sector. However, we must also be cognizant of the macroeconomic environment the company operates in and the fact that regional banks are very cyclical; that is, they are sensitive to the business cycle. The current headwinds cannot be ignored and make entering into new positions a bleak endeavor at this time. The headwinds are as follows:

Regional banks perform best when the yield curve is steep and upward-sloping, in other words, when rates on the short end are lower than rates on the long end of the curve. As of the time of this writing, the market has essentially priced out all rate cuts that were priced in prior to the Iran war. Per the CME FedWatch tool, fed funds futures are pricing an approximate 71% probability of no cuts, only a 3.7% chance of one 25 basis point cut by then, and even 25% in rate hikes.

Additionally, the deterioration in the private credit market has elevated fears of a contagion throughout the broader market. Regional banks are particularly exposed to this potential contagion as the primary lenders to private credit funds. In fact, the Federal Reserve stated that credit lines by U.S. banks to private credit vehicles increased about 145% over the prior five years in its note titled "Bank Lending to Private Credit: Size, Characteristics, and Financial Stability Implications." These private credit funds then use "back leverage" to enhance returns. This practice refers to borrowing against a pool of loans from banks, which can work well in good times but may sting when times are bad.

The issue is particularly acute for regional banks, as these types of loans make up a larger portion of their balance sheets relative to the big banks, per a research piece from Capital Advisors titled "Are Regional Banks Sitting on A Private Credit Time Bomb?" For instance, the research piece specifically cites a loss linked to Tricolor for $178 million at Fifth Third (FITB). The loans to private credit could cause significant issues for regional banks in the event of an economic stress scenario if pressure from investor redemptions forces them to rely more heavily on bank credit lines for liquidity at the same time collateral values may be falling, causing banks to pull back on these facilities, further stressing the private credit funds.

In case this wasn't enough of a cause for concern, a stress in the private credit market may threaten bank capital if the credit quality of these loans suddenly falls. Banks are required to hold regulatory capital in proportion to their risk-weighted assets; a larger portion of "high-risk" assets generally forces a bank to hold more capital. While these loans to private credit funds may currently be considered "low-risk," if the credit quality deteriorates, they may be elevated to high-risk, forcing banks to shore up more capital to meet regulatory capital requirements, which would mean less capital deployed to grow the business.

Finally, signs are emerging of stress in consumer credit as evidenced by three key points. First, credit card delinquencies (90+ days past due) are approaching prior cycle peaks at 7.18%, per the St. Louis Fed's Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit. Additionally, student loan balances that have entered serious delinquency have risen to 16.19%, which may force borrowers to prioritize payments on these loans at the expense of other loans.

Lastly, stress is emerging in the Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) market as a report from LendingTree titled "BNPL Tracker: 41% of Users Late in Past Year, More Using Loans for Groceries" shows that 41% of users have paid late on these loans, up from 34% a year ago. While Wintrust does not specifically provide financing for these types of loans, broad credit issues like this can impact banks as consumers may have to forego payments on other loans.

Valuation

For valuation, we will use the price-to-tangible book value (P/TBV) and the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio for backup.

First, the P/TBV ratio is the preferred metric for banks as it controls for intangible assets like goodwill, which can arise through non-core activities like mergers, which are common in the banking industry. On a P/TBV ratio level, WTFC trades at an approximate 2% discount to its justified price of $138.32 based on the peer median of 1.56 and the current price of $135.33 as of the time of this writing.

Second, the P/E ratio is a suitable backup to the P/TBV ratio; however, it is important to note that this metric can be distorted by the PCL, which we reviewed earlier. On a P/E ratio level, WTFC trades at almost a 3% premium to its justified price of $131.36, based on the peer median of 10.43. This shows why it is helpful to have a backup metric for valuing complex firms like banks.

Taking the midpoint of the justified price from the P/TBV ratio and the P/E ratio, we arrive at a fair price of $138.32, or an approximate 2% upside from the current price of $135.33. Suffice it to say this is well within the margin of error for calculating fair value; therefore, Wintrust appears to be trading at its fair value.

Conclusion

Wintrust is a high-quality player in the regional banking space that has experienced strong growth excellent deposit growth and uses a multi-charter business model that has grown the firm significantly. However, a yield curve squeezing net interest margin, the possibility of a private credit contagion, and falling demand for loanable funds make this the wrong time to enter into new positions on regional banks. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on Wintrust.

data.title: Funko: Avoid This Stock As Sales Pull Back Further
data.description:
In tough macroeconomic environments, weak companies often sink. Amid oil-driven inflation and a still-weak consumer spending environment, we're cautious about struggling companies like Funko (FNKO), the maker of Pop! collectible figurines.

Since the start of the year, shares of Funko have lost ~15% of their value, the culmination of a very tough year for the stock in which it has lost about 60% of its value over the past twelve months. The question for investors now is, is this a chance to buy the dip in Funko, or is the company permanently damaged?

I last wrote a sell article on Funko in December, when the stock was trading in the low $3s. Since then, the stock has proceeded to lose value. At the same time, it's hard to point to anything in its fundamentals that can justify a recovery. I remain at a sell rating here.

To me, there are quite a few red flags that this company faces that investors should be fully aware of. The core risk factors are:

  • Funko is facing a very weak sales environment. Revenue has declined at a persistent double-digit pace. The company is facing weak consumer spending and a cautious retail partner environment as well. Also, since Funko focuses on current trends and fads, incorrectly planning supply could cause issues with obsolete inventory. Inventory write-offs have recently crimped the company's gross margin profile.
  • Tariffs are eating into gross margins. Funko already suffers from relatively weak <50% gross margins that will ultimately cap its ability to scale. Tariffs are pushing down margins further, with ~4 points of negative impact in the most recent quarter.
  • Moderately indebted. The company is carrying about $224 million in gross debt ($182 million in net debt) that sits at a ~2.4x leverage ratio. Amid falling sales and waning profitability, the company doesn't have much flexibility if sales slip further.

It's true that Funko's valuation is modest in nominal terms, but considering the operational risks the company is shouldering, I find its multiples to be fairly priced at best. At current share prices just shy of $3, Funko trades at a market cap of $162.5 million. After we net off the $42.1 million of cash and $224.1 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Funko's resulting enterprise value is $344.5 million.

For FY26, Funko is guiding to 0-3% sales growth and $70-80 million in adjusted EBITDA, or an 8.1% margin. We caution that this represents vast improvement over 2.9% margins in FY25 (though not quite yet at FY24 levels at a 9.0% margin), despite the fact that sales deleverage, obsolete inventory, and tariffs will still factor in as headwinds next year.

Nevertheless, if we take this guidance at face value, Funko trades at 4.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. Again, this isn't a terrible price to pay, but considering the recent slippage in sales and various operating risks in a tough economy, this isn't a bet I would make.

Q4 Recap

Let's now go through Funko's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Funko's revenue declined -7% y/y to $273.1 million, which did beat Wall Street's expectations of $260.7 million (-11% y/y) by a four-point margin. Revenue trends did improve somewhat relative to a -15% y/y decline in Q3.

Still, it's a long road to recovery for Funko. The chart below showcases Funko's sales trends by product line. As shown below, the company saw flat trends in its Loungefly accessories business. However, core collectibles saw a -$12 million decline in revenue. Certain properties did well: the company notes that sales of items branded for KPop Demon Hunters and Stranger Things, both of which are core recent titles on Netflix (NFLX), did well. That said, overall sales weakness was still pronounced, especially in the U.S. The company also separately buckets sales losses from SKUs that it has discontinued in its "Other" line, though I continue to disagree with this categorization (as I'm of the belief that if these SKUs had been present, they might have cannibalized against products in the core collection).

Unfortunately, however, Funko's profitability has been trending weaker. As shown in the chart below, pro forma gross margins fell -150bps y/y to 40.9%.

The good news is that underlying product margins improved 370bps y/y, while a greater mix of full-price DTC sales has also helped the company's margin profile. Unfortunately, this was entirely offset by -410bps of negative headwind from tariffs. On top of this, the company suffered a -140bps hit to gross margins from inventory reserves: essentially unsold inventory that the company has had to mark down. As previously mentioned, due to Funko's reliance on current trends to drive sales of figurines, weak demand and poor supply planning could continue to be a major drag on the bottom line.

Adjusted EBITDA also fell -11% y/y to $23.3 million, falling to an 8.5% adjusted EBITDA margin that reduced -40bps y/y.

For now, the company is fairly confident that it doesn't need to dip into additional debt to fund its declining profits. Per CFO Yves LePendeven's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

So the answer is no, we don't expect to need to do any additional borrowing. We're managing right now on our operating cash flows and expect to continue to do so. We make regular quarterly principal and interest payments on the debt, and we plan potentially later in this year and certainly early next year to make some incremental debt paydowns like we did in Q4."

Yet we note that free cash flow in FY25 was -$38 million, and the company has only $42.1 million of cash left on its books. If the weak macro pushes sales trends even lower in FY26, we may see Funko enter into a deeper cash crunch.

Key Takeaways

While Funko's low-single-digit adjusted EBITDA multiples look tempting, I believe Funko is a classic value trap. The company hasn't been able to solve its multi-quarter problem of declining sales while facing a tough macro and eroding margins. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere

data.title: Argan: At Highs, But Strong Earnings Momentum Keeps The Bull Case Intact
data.description:
The Thesis

Exiting FY26, a key provider of power and industrial infrastructure services, Argan, Inc. (AGX), posted a strong double-beat result in Q4, reflecting continued demand momentum and solid project execution. While demand for the company's capabilities remains robust across the power infrastructure, with rising data center investments and electrification trends, additional support from a strong $2.9 billion backlog should drive top line growth through FY27.

For margins, I believe focusing on disciplined execution on large-scale projects and operational efficiencies should be a key growth factor over the coming quarters, supporting bottom line growth in FY27 and beyond as the top line expands. Since my last bullish rating, the AGX stock has given strong double-digit returns, driven by solid earnings performance in Q4.

While the recent rally has expanded the valuation notably and now looks relatively stretched on FY27 earnings estimates, it still appears reasonable due to the company's strong earnings momentum, robust growth outlook, and favorable position in an industry with long term structural tailwinds. The risk-reward indeed looks more balanced at the present levels, but I believe the company continues to offer a compelling opportunity for long-term investors, supporting a continued buy rating on the stock.

AGX’s Q4 FY26 Highlights

Earlier this week, AGX reported its final quarter results for FY26, closing the year strongly with approximately 12.7% year on year growth across the company’s consolidated top line, reaching $262 million in Q4 FY26. This growth was primarily driven by the completion of certain large projects during the quarter, which benefited the revenue in the company’s core Power Services segment.

Although a smaller contributor, AGX’s Industrial construction segment also benefited the company's revenue growth notably, expanding by about 61% to $52.9 million during the quarter. For the full year, the consolidated top line was up 8.1% versus FY25, inching closer to the billion-dollar mark, at approximately $944.6 million.

While the company’s top line continues to expand, it also remains focused on strong project execution, which significantly benefited its profitability during the last quarter. As a result, the company’s adjusted EBITDA margin jumped about 450 bps to a record 21.4% in Q4. EBITDA growth also meaningfully boosted the company’s bottom line, as its adjusted EPS jumped nearly 60% year on year to $3.47, beating the estimates by a notable $1.49 in Q4 FY26.

Comments on Outlook

After experiencing a slight moderation during the middle of FY26 due to unfavorable project timing, AGX closed the year on a solid note with an impressive Q4, primarily driven by healthy growth in the core Power service segment. While there was some temporary softness driven by project timing earlier, a solid Q4 reinforces the company’s underlying growth trajectory. I expect this momentum to continue into FY27 as the demand environment for the company’s capabilities across the segments remains strong.

The power services segment, which remains the core contributor with about 80% share in total revenue, continues to see strong demand from increasing requirements for new power generation capacity across the region. While AI-led data centers continue to expand rapidly, I expect the requirement for new capacity to rise further to serve the need for reliable baseload power infrastructure across North America.

This strong demand environment can also be seen in the company’s project activity throughout FY26, with new contracts during the year reaching $2.5 billion. These include both natural gas and renewable projects. However, about 77% of them are natural gas related, which includes three large gas-fired power plant projects in the U.S. with a combined capacity of about 3.4 GW. With this strong order activity, the company’s consolidated backlog stands at approximately $2.93 billion as of January 2026, which provides solid visibility for the company’s top line growth as it progresses through FY27.

Additionally, the company also has many large projects that are progressing steadily. These include the 1.2 GW combined-cycle natural gas project in Texas and a 700 MW combined-cycle gas-fired project. While these projects are advancing through their later stages, I expect them to further contribute to the company’s revenue growth meaningfully over the coming quarters and support the company’s consolidated top line in FY27.

AGX’s outlook beyond FY27 also looks equally promising due to structural tailwinds from rising investments in data centers, electrification trends across industries, and onshoring of manufacturing. Given years of underinvestment in power generation capacity across the region, the need for new and reliable energy infrastructure is increasing. This is likely to fuel a multi-year investment cycle in power infrastructure across North America, which should significantly benefit companies like AGX due to their strong execution capabilities and expertise in complex projects.

AGX also has a long-standing customer relationship with a great track record. In addition to its capabilities, I believe strong customer relationships should further strengthen its position in capitalizing on future project opportunities over the coming years.

Furthermore, AGX also continues to focus on enhancing its capabilities and expanding its footprint through strategic M&As. With approximately $895 million in cash and equivalents and absolutely no debt, the company’s financials also remain strong, which provides it with the flexibility for future M&A opportunities, which should further benefit its top line in FY27 and beyond.

Valuation Update

I last covered the AGX stock in December 2025, after it reported its Q3 results for FY26. While the stock corrected nearly 20% post-earnings release amid concerns around top line weakness and a revenue miss in Q3 earnings, it was still trading at a forward multiple around 38x its FY26 earnings at that time.

Despite the short term softness, I remained bullish, as the weakness appeared largely project timing-related rather than structural. And, as expected, the company delivered a strong double beat in its Q4 FY26 earnings, significantly exceeding Wall Street expectations, leading to a sharp post-earnings rally of about 38%. As a result, the stock is now up about 74% since my last buy call, significantly outperforming the S&P 500, which has been down 7.5% during the same period.

However, after the recent surge, AGX no longer appears to be an undervalued growth story. The company's stock is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 51.92, based on its FY27 earnings estimates of $10.91. While the stock appears significantly overvalued versus its five-year forward average multiple of around 23x, it is important to note that AGX is not a typical EPC company. But it is positioned as a more focused pure play EPC company with limited direct peers and strong capabilities.

Plus, the valuation is also supported by strong forward growth expectations, driven by a robust project backlog and a favorable demand environment. Due to these factors, I believe the market may continue to assign a premium multiple to AGX relative to traditional EPC peers as long as it continues to execute efficiently and sustain double-digit growth over the medium term.

Moving ahead, AGX's top line outlook appears favorable as strong backlog levels and ramp-up of large projects keep the visibility strong into FY27. Meanwhile, with continued focus on strong execution and improving project management effectiveness, the company's margin should also remain strong over the coming quarters.

Importantly, the company's debt-free balance sheet should also help the company translate the benefit from top line and margin expansion largely into bottom line expansion, which should benefit the company's stock valuation in the future. Additionally, I also believe that the probability of further upward earnings revision in consensus earnings estimates also remains high, given strong and consistent earnings momentum, which is also likely to help normalize the current multiple over time.

Overall, at the present levels, the risk reward appears more balanced after the recent rally, and near term upside may be relatively limited. But, given the company's strong earnings momentum and position in a high-growth market, I still believe that AGX remains a high-quality compounder for the long term.

Risks

Similar to my previous article, lumpiness in AGX's top line remains a key risk to the thesis. As we have seen before as well, these uneven project timings could negatively impact the company's top line in the short term despite solid underlying demand. In addition, project execution risks, including the cost overrun in large-scale projects, also remain a key factor to watch.

Also, as the stock is currently trading at a premium valuation, there is almost no room for any execution issues. Given the elevated expectation, if the company could not deliver as expected in the coming quarter results, or growth moderates, the company's stock might see a near term correction..

Final Verdict and Rating

As discussed above, the company's stock is now trading at a stretched valuation following the recent surge. The company's outlook for FY27 appears solid with strong project visibility and strong margin levels, which should continue to support earnings growth over the coming quarters. Although the risk-reward looks more balanced now, AGX's strong market positioning and strong execution continue to support a constructive view, given a promising long term outlook. Therefore, while some caution is warranted at present levels, I remain bullish on AGX and believe that any short-term volatility should be viewed as an opportunity for investors with a long-term view rather than a concern. Hence, I am maintaining a "BUY" rating on the AGX stock.

data.title: Capital One's Series I Preferred Stock Offers Decent Yield And Potential Capital Gain
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Capital One Financial Corporation (COF) has several outstanding series of preferred stock (all of which are pari passu), including two non-publicly traded series. Their five series of publicly traded preferred stock each consist of depositary shares representing 1/40th interest in a share of fixed-rate, non-cumulative, perpetual preferred stock, as shown in the following table.

The Series I Preferred Stock (COF.PR.I) is trading at a higher yield (and thus a lower price) than is reasonable for its risk level, particularly as it is trading significantly below its liquidation preference of $25 and, with the highest coupon of Capital One's publicly traded series and the presently callable series, is the most likely of the outstanding preferred stock series to be called anytime soon (particularly if the Federal Reserve cuts rates, thus opening up the possibility of refinancing this series at a cheaper coupon). This implies a good chance of significant capital gains if it gets called. Given the major, global economic shocks in the commodity and financial markets related to the new war on Iran as well as the chaotic on-again/off-again trade wars, there is risk to COF's consumer-based lending if the economy falls into recession (Q4 2025's GDP growth was revised downward from 1.4% to 0.7%). While this may impact Capital One's common stock's fortunes on the downside (as I discuss in a recent review of Capital One's common stock), COF.PR.I should be relatively shielded from impact on its dividend. I rate COF.PR.I as a buy.

Company Overview

Founded in 1988 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia, Capital One Financial Corporation (the "Company") is the holding company of Capital One, NA, a bank primarily focusing on consumer and commercial banking. The company's customer deposits fund its lending, with its loan-to-deposit ratio ("LDR") ranging from 90% to 96% over the past five years (at the end of FY2025, after the merger with Discover, LDR was 96%). According to its profile on Seeking Alpha, the Company operates over three segments: Credit Cards, Consumer Banking, and Commercial Banking. Deposits consist of savings accounts, checking accounts, money market deposits, negotiable orders of withdrawal, time deposits, and sweep accounts. It offers credit card loans, personal loans, auto loans, retail banking loans, commercial and multifamily real estate loans, and commercial and industrial loans.

The largest event impacting the Company over the last five years was its $35.3 billion acquisition of Discover Financial Services, finalized on May 18, 2025. It was an all-stock deal for 1.0192 shares of COF for each Discover share. This was followed by a notice on January 22, 2026 that the Company was acquiring Brex for a combination of stock and cash worth $5.15 billion. While the Company may very well benefit in the long run from these acquisitions, for the near term it appears that the Company will have a tough climb to return to its five-year historical average return on equity ("ROE") of 11.53%.

Review of the Series I Preferred Stock

As shown in the following table, COF.PR.I have the highest coupon (5.0%) of all five of Capital One's publicly traded preferred stock series outstanding. It also exceeds the coupon of the non-publicly traded Series M preferred stock. Only the non-publicly traded Series O preferred stock has a higher coupon (5.50%), but its call date is not until October 30, 2027.

COF.PR.I's annual dividend of $1.25 is paid out on a quarterly basis (March 1, June 1, September 1, and December 1), the same as Capital One's other preferred stock series except Series O, which is paid twice yearly until October 30, 2027, after which it will be paid quarterly. At $18.60, Series I is currently trading at a 25.6% discount to its liquidation preference of $25.00. This is a lower discount than the other four publicly traded series, which ranged from a 29.4% discount to a 37.0% discount. COF.PR.I's current yield of 6.720% is just below the other publicly tradable preferred stock series. This is likely because it has the highest coupon and is also the most likely callable series of Capital One's publicly traded preferred stock.

All five of the publicly traded preferred stock series are fixed rate, non-cumulative, perpetual preferred stock. In order for preferred stock to be counted as Tier 1 capital, it must be non-cumulative, so financial institutions generally issue non-cumulative perpetual preferred stock. As with all non-cumulative preferred stock, dividends are paid only when, as, and if declared by the respective institutions' board of directors. These dividends have precedence over the dividends paid to common stockholders.

Capital One's Ratio Analysis

I took a look at historical ratios (calculated using Capital One's 10-Ks and 10-Qs) to give me an indication of how Capital One has performed, which will show the safety of COF.PR.I's dividend and its liquidation value of $25 per depositary share. A series of ratios on profitability, financial strength, liquidity, and efficiency, all using figures as reported, are shown in the following table.

Net interest margin measures the difference between interest income and interest expenses, with a higher margin being better. Capital One has grown its net interest margin annually from 5.66% in FY2021 to 7.40% in FY2025.

ROE shows how much net income is generated by the average common shareholder's equity. Please note, net income is what remains after income attributable to the non-controlling interests (such as preferred stock) is paid out. A higher ROE shows that there is a cushion of earnings to the common equity that is above and beyond the dividends for the preferred stock, thus leaving room for lower earnings to the common shares without sacrificing the dividend of the preferred stock. ROE has declined annually, with FY2021 having an ROE of 21.45% and FY2025 having an ROE of 2.66%. This is significantly below COF's cost of (common) equity of 9.14%, found using the Capital Asset Pricing Model ("CAPM").

Return on Assets ("ROA") measures net income against average total assets of Capital One. This measure also declined annually from 2.80% in FY2021 to 0.38% in FY2025.

The provision for credit losses ratio shows the amount of provisions for credit losses as a percentage of net loans. This number increased annually from 4.21% in FY2021 to 5.43% in FY2025. The provision for credit losses measure also reduces net income and increases the allowance for credit losses, a contra-asset account on the balance sheet.

Several ratios for financial strength were reviewed, with higher percentages showing greater financial strength. The Tier 1 leverage ratio shows a bank's core capital to its total assets. The Common Equity Tier 1 ("CET1") risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's common equity (i.e., exclusive of preferred stock and qualifying minority interests) against its risk-weighted assets. The Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's equity capital and disclosed reserves against its total risk-weighted assets. Finally, total risk-based capital ratio shows the sum of Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital divided by the bank's risk-weighted assets. All these measures for Capital One are significantly above regulatory minimums for all years reviewed.

It should be noted that the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation ("FDIC") approved new rules in November 2025 easing leverage requirements for banks, requiring less capital set aside to protect against losses of lower-risk assets (like US Treasuries).

The company's LDR is a percentage of how much of the company's deposits are utilized to fund loans. Having a ratio below 100% means all the bank's lending is funded internally, whereas a ratio higher than 100% means the bank is dependent on outside financing (e.g., through raising funds from new sales of preferred stock) to finance its growth. Capital One's LDR ranged from a low of 90% in FY2024 to a high of 96% in FY2025. This leaves some room for Capital One to increase its lending backed by deposits.

The efficiency ratio shows non-interest expenses per dollar of revenue, with a lower ratio being better. Looking at FY2021 through FY2024, this ratio ranged from a low of 54.44% in FY2021 to a high of 55.95% in FY2022. In FY2025, it increased to 57.08%.

Revenue growth less non-interest expense growth, when positive, shows that revenue is growing faster than expenses, as opposed to a negative ratio, which shows the company is accumulating expenses faster than it is growing revenues. This figure ranged from a low of negative 13.87% in FY2021 to a high of positive 1.39% in FY2023, only to then decline over the next two years to end at negative 5.33% in FY2025.

As with all preferred stock, COF.PR.I ranks below debt and above the common stock, COF, in terms of liquidation preference and dividend payments. As of December 31, 2025, COF's (i.e., the common stock's) book value per share is $181.76, and its tangible book value (book value less goodwill and intangible assets) per share is $107.72. COF's current market price is $176.16, which has fallen from $241.44 on December 31, 2025. Despite this decline, there is a large cushion of common equity that can be drawn down before resorting to lowering COF.PR.I's equity stake.

In summary, these ratios show that while Capital One fares favorably in its financial strength and liquidity ratios, it has some issues with its profitability as well as its efficiency, particularly after its acquisition of Discover Financial in FY2025.

Preferred Stock Comparables

A sample of preferred stocks issued by financial institutions of similar asset size was reviewed and compared to COF.PR.I. I left out JPMorgan Chase Bank (due to its size), Goldman Sachs (as it has minimal consumer finance business), State Street (it had no outstanding preferred securities), and TD Bank (which is denominated in Canadian dollars, so not comparable to the others). See the following table taken from the Federal Reserve.

I also included a search for preferred securities issued by Capital One's peers, as listed on Seeking Alpha. To winnow down the results, I excluded cumulative securities, floating-rate securities, fixed-to-floating-rate securities, and short-dated rate-reset preferred stock. This search resulted in 26 comparable securities, as shown on the following table.

Whether the preferred shares were trading at a premium to or a discount to their liquidation prices shows whether a higher yield is demanded by the market than the coupon issued by these companies. Most of these preferred stocks traded at a discount to their liquidation preferences, with a handful of preferred stocks trading at a premium to their liquidation preferences. These preferred stocks ranged from a 40.1% discount to liquidation preference to a 2.4% premium to liquidation preference, with a mean and median discount to liquidation preference of 19.4% and 24.1%, respectively. COF.PR.I currently trades at a 25.6% discount to its liquidation preference (slightly above the median).

The yields on these securities ranged from a low of 5.97% to a high of 7.52%, with a mean and median of 6.28% and 6.26%, respectively. COF.PR.I's yield (as traded) is 6.72%. It should be noted that if the single preferred stock issued by Synchrony Financial was excluded from the sample, the maximum yield of the sample dropped to 6.47%, with the mean and median being 6.23% and 6.26%, respectively. That puts Capital One's Series I preferred stock, as traded, at a higher yield than the adjusted sample.

Most of these securities, including Capital One's Series I preferred stock, were already past their call dates and thus had no meaningful yield to call. It should be noted that several securities in the sample had very large yields to call. This occurred when they traded at a significant discount to their liquidation preference and were only a few coupon payments away from their call dates. If called, they would receive the full $25 call price, thus having a large capital gain at call, inflating the yield earned for holding these securities to call.

At $53,434 million, Capital One had lower revenue than all the other financial institutions in the sample, except for U.S. Bancorp, Synchrony Financial, Truist Financial Corporation, and Bank of New York Mellon Corporation. Revenues ranged from $14,981 million to $113,097 million, with the mean and median revenue being $72,339 million (just above Capital One's revenue) and $83,699 million, respectively.

In terms of net income available to the common shareholders (i.e., a cushion to losses that needs to be zeroed out before dividends to the preferred shareholders are cut), it ranged from a low of $3,469 million to a high of $29,055 million, with a mean and median of $17,669 million and $18,267 million, respectively. Capital One's net income available to common shareholders was $2,181 million, i.e., below the group's minimum. This would imply a higher yield for the Series I preferred stock than the comparable sample.

I also looked at the net income margins of the sample, which ranged from a low of 18.89% to a high of 36.41%. This was well above Capital One's net income margin of 7.48%. A similar pattern was seen for ROA and ROE, with the sample's minimums being 0.54% and 6.83%, respectively, while Capital One's ROA and ROE were 0.37% and 2.39%, respectively. These differences would imply that Capital One's Series I preferred stock should trade at a higher yield than the sample.

All these factors should be considered together when determining a yield for a security. In particular, the likelihood of the Series I stock being called before any of the other preferred stocks issued by Capital One implies its yield should be lower than other Capital One preferred stocks. However, given the comparison to the metrics of the sample, the Series I preferred stock should trade at a yield towards the high end of the sample. Taking all of this into account, I selected a reasonable yield of 6.50% for the Series I preferred stock, just below its 6.72% yield it is currently trading at. This implies a preferred stock price of $19.23, a 23.1% discount to its liquidation price. This compares favorably to its currently traded price of $18.60.

Risks & Other Factors

According to Capital One, the Discover acquisition created a global payments platform with over 70 million merchant acceptance points in more than 200 countries and territories. The Company cites $2.7 billion in pre-tax synergies and, by 2027, that it will be >15% accretive to adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share. Their 2027 return on invested capital is projected to be 16%, with an internal rate of return of >20%. These projections are potential positives for the company; however, the company's declining ROA and ROE in the past five years show multiple warning signs that these projections might not come about. Additionally, the company's concentration in consumer lending typically carries a risk of loss that is higher than commercial lending and can be adversely affected by an economic downturn. The likelihood of an economic downturn increases substantially when trade wars heat up, as they are currently, or if the independence of the Federal Reserve comes into question. The war in Iran has also roiled commodity and financial markets, and it is unclear how long this factor will negatively influence the stock market as well as the overall economy.

Conclusion

Given all the preceding information, I believe that even with the potential downside to Capital One's common stock highlighted in my earlier article, its dividends to its preferred securities should be relatively safe. As a result, I estimated a yield for the Series I preferred stock of 6.50% with an implied stock price of $19.23. Any future rate cuts by the Federal Reserve will further boost the preferred stock's price and simultaneously make it more likely to be called, as Capital One could then refinance this higher coupon security for a lower coupon one. I rate COF.PR.I as a buy.

data.title: TopBuild's Plunge Isn't Time For An Upgrade
data.description:
The last few months have been a difficult time for shareholders of TopBuild (BLD). After I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate back in November of last year, shares of the business fell 16.3%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is down 4.1%. This is disconcerting to say the least. And it comes at a time when financial performance for the business is mixed. Revenue is actually rising, in large part because of acquisition activities. But profitability has, for the most part, declined. Long term, the company is positioned for success. But short-term concerns, not to mention how the stock is priced, justify more of a ‘hold’ rating than they do a ‘buy.’ Even though the stock is underperforming the market since I last wrote about it, that underperformance is smaller when we look at it from the time I originally rated it a ‘hold’ back in July of 2023. In that time, shares have risen 34.6%. That is below the 48.9% increase that the S&P 500 saw. But it's not drastically lower. My overall assessment is that it will continue to perform more or less along the lines of the market moving forward. And because of that, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ makes the most sense.

Taking a Fresh Look at TopBuild

Operationally speaking, I find TopBuild to be a fascinating business. Through the more than 200 installation service branches that it has throughout the US, as well as the more than 150 distribution centers that it boasts throughout North America, it operates as a provider of various solutions, most notably the installation of insulation. It also engages in services associated with the construction and repair of commercial roofs, the distribution of shingles, and other construction product distribution activities like those involving rain gutters.

In my previous article about the company, I talked about how a sizable housing shortage in the US would eventually bode well for the company. I also detailed management's plans to grow revenue from what we now know was $5.41 billion in 2025 to between $9 billion and $10 billion by 2030. But I didn't really talk about the absolute magnitude of the space that it plays in. Management estimates that the total addressable market for the company stands at around $90 billion. $75 billion of this is the commercial roofing installation space.

By comparison, other market segments are much smaller. The residential building envelope insulation installation and distribution market, as well as the commercial building envelope insulation installation and distribution market, are each worth about $6.25 billion. And the mechanical and metal building insulation fabricated insulation and distribution market is worth only about $6 billion. Large market, the company has been very active in buying up other businesses. Since 2022, the firm has spent more than $2.2 billion on mergers and acquisitions activities. The 24 purchases that it made from the end of 2022 until late 2025 added $1.5 billion in annual revenue to the company. And when you add on top of this certain benefits like synergies, it's clear that the company has benefited from these activities.

Acquisitions have absolutely helped the company grow, even recently. In the final quarter of 2025, as an example, revenue amounted to $1.49 billion. That's a sizable increase over the $1.31 billion that the company reported a year earlier. For the company as a whole, actual organic sales volume dropped 10.5% year over year. And price increases helped to offset this only to the tune of 0.7%. It was actually through mergers and acquisitions activities that sales were able to increase by 13.2%, with those adding 23% to the top line for the company. And for the year as a whole, the 8.8% benefit that the company got from acquisition activities more than offset the 8.1% drop in sales volumes that the company saw on an organic basis.

Unfortunately, profitability for the company took a hit. Net income went from $150.5 million to $104.5 million. But that's not all. Operating cash flow for the company contracted from $266.2 million to $176.7 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $190.5 million to $155 million. The only increase that we saw came from EBITDA. And that was modest, with the metric growing from $258 million to $265.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. As I mentioned already, mergers and acquisitions activities did the heavy lifting here, pushing the company up quite a bit. Without this, revenue would have actually fallen year over year. But once again, profitability took a hit. Even EBITDA declined during this window of time. According to management, the overall hit that the company took in terms of profitability came from two primary categories.

For starters, for 2025 at least, the cost of sales rose from 69.5% of revenue to 71%. This was blamed on the reduction in sales volumes and customer price pressures on residential products associated with its distribution business. As I have detailed in other articles, the residential construction market is under pressure here. High interest rates following years of inflationary pressures have made homes unaffordable for many Americans. So it stands to reason that those clients would push back out of necessity. In addition to this, selling, general, and administrative costs rose from 13.9% of sales to 14.4%. This was a mixed bag, though it was largely because of acquisition activities. After all, in 2025 alone, the company spent $1.94 billion on acquisitions to add $1.2 billion of annual revenue. Integration and closing costs do not come cheap.

A lot of what the company is doing is centered around its Specialty Distribution segment, through which the company sells certain insulation and insulation accessories, like fiberglass and spray foam. It might seem odd that this part of the business, which, as the image below illustrates, brings in lower margins than the Installation Services segment, is a big focus of growth for the company. After all, focusing on the Installation Services side of the company might make more sense. However, management firmly believes that there exist strong cross-selling opportunities between these operations. Considering the distribution network that I defined already, this isn't shocking to me.

When it comes to 2026, management has provided some guidance. They expect, for instance, for revenue to come in at between $5.925 billion and $6.225 billion. It looks as though the firm will suffer from a mid-single-digit decline in residential sales that will be only partially addressed through a low-single-digit growth rate associated with commercial and industrial sales. The heavy lifting will, once again, come from mergers and acquisitions, which will add between $800 million and $850 million in revenue for the company. If the firm does achieve these targets, then net profits should range between $446 million and $568 million. The midpoint there would be down from what the company saw in 2025. Meanwhile, EBITDA is expected to be between $1.005 billion and $1.115 billion. The midpoint would be a bit above the $1.04 billion that the company saw last year. And if the company does hit that target, we are probably looking at about $710.1 million of adjusted operating cash flow.

Using these estimates, as well as the results the company saw in 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. These aren't bad multiples. But they aren't fantastic either. But when you add on top of this my own view that economic conditions are deteriorating, then these multiples definitely are not low enough to justify a bullish outlook. This is especially true when you consider how the stock is priced compared to shares of other similar firms. In the aforementioned chart, I compared it to five other similar companies as well. As you can tell, the company is around the middle of the group in relation to these. And in fact, when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, it is near the high end of the range.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my assertion that TopBuild is fine, but it's far from great. I am concerned about the state of the economy, and I believe that will weigh on the business more than anticipated. This doesn't mean that I expect financial performance to worsen. Management is clearly of the opinion that some metrics will improve year over year. But considering how shares are priced, I think that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ is the right choice here.

data.title: JD.com: Even If It Stops Growing, It Still Is A Fundamental Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It's often heard nowadays that inefficiencies are more often found outside the US, and this time, I believe this seems to be proven right.

JD.com (JD) is one of the biggest and most popular online retailers in China. As of late, there is significant pessimism surrounding it, mainly due to the high competition within its industry. JD.com must compete with strong players such as Alibaba Group, PDD Holdings, Suning.com, and Vipshop in China, and with Coupang in South Korea and global giant Amazon outside China.

While the company had experienced a lot of growth from 2016 to 2021, growth has somewhat decelerated in the last few years. The company also reported disappointing operating margins of 0.28% for 2025, a margin growth deceleration of -90.27% compared to 2024. What is the main culprit? An increase of 76.16% in SGA expenses and R&D spending increase of 36.26% in AI initiatives and software development for customer satisfaction improvement and increase of partner productivity.

A softening in consumer demand has also prevented JD from reaching pre-pandemic revenue growth, and while consumers are not distressed, they are more cautious about spending and are choosing to save money instead.

The company also continues to invest heavily in its core business and in different projects (e.g., food delivery business), and investors are convinced these will be successful for the company and the investors over the long term.

Despite the current headwinds and the stock sell-off, I believe the market is underestimating JD's potential and current economic worth. The company seems to be trying to develop a moat through economies of scale by investing heavily in infrastructure, technology, and acquisitions. The company is aiming to become the infrastructure (online and offline) that makes e-commerce possible in China. While this is an interesting proposition, what I like most about this idea is that, regardless of whether this strategy is going to work in the long term, the market is underestimating the current true economic value of JD.com when we consider the net asset value (NAV) of the company and the earnings power value (EPV).

The company has roughly $47 billion between cash and non-core assets, according to the most recent financial statements. The company also has intangible assets worth around $27 billion, which are distributed between its product portfolio, a labor force of 900,000 full-time and part-time workers, and a highly valuable customer base. The company has low debt (relative to equity), and it's maintained a credit rating of A-. I believe the market is underappreciating the current net asset value of its assets, and combined with a conservative estimate of sustainable earnings power, JD.com is currently undervalued by around 32.3%. So even if the company stopped growing revenues, an investment in JD.com still offers a return of ~47.7% (if we buy at the current price of $28.70 per share) when we use these valuation metrics based on current fundamentals.

Company Overview

Founded in January 2004 by Richard Qiangdong Liu, JD.com sits today as one of the most popular online retailers from China, with more than 700M active users. The company focuses on developing its online retail business as well as building its own fulfillment infrastructure, which includes last-mile delivery capability, based on its proprietary technology platform to support its operations. After its online retail business grew, it launched its online marketplace that allowed third-party merchants to sell its products using JD's infrastructure. More recently, the company started to offer comprehensive supply chain-based services to complement its core business and create "significant value" for its wide range of business partners.

Besides its retail business, the company also has other subsidiaries such as JD Health, JD Property, JD Industrials, and Dada. These other businesses are only a small fraction of JD's total revenue. JD Logistics, another subsidiary, is a leading industrial supply chain technology and service provider in China, and it accounts for a substantial portion of JD's total revenues, being its second most profitable business.

The pessimism surrounding JD is not entirely unjustified. The company was expected to be a compounder, but the poor performance of the company in recent years has disappointed investors. As we know, companies with no economic moat don't produce excess returns above the cost of capital, and the growth these companies may achieve won't be value-creating growth. My valuation work will prove that, fundamentally speaking, JD doesn't have a current economic moat, and for companies with no moat, the value of growth is negligible in the valuation process, and we can estimate its value using the NAV and EPV models. With these parameters set, the value of JD is substantially higher than the current market gap, and with the current development and investment in infrastructure, there is optionality in this investment since there is always the possibility that JD will be able to leverage those assets and produce excess returns in the future.

Valuation Analysis

Companies that have durable economic moats tend to have extremely high excess returns, and therefore, the value of growth must be considered when coming up with an estimate of intrinsic value. However, in highly competitive markets, where companies don't have real economic moats, intrinsic value can be measured using the NAV and EPV model. That's exactly what I'm going to do, and my model is going to prove two things. (1) JD doesn't show signs of having an economic moat that allows it to produce excess returns to its shareholders. (2) JD is nonetheless fundamentally undervalued, thus making it an attractive value investment.

Net Asset Value

To find the net asset value of JD, it's necessary to analyze the latest balance sheet and compare the book value of its assets and liabilities and compare it to the current reproduction cost to come up with an estimate of the NAV in equity terms (and then in enterprise value terms).

Current assets are assumed to have reproduction costs similar to their book values, since they can generally be turned into cash within a year. For inventories, JD.com uses an accounting method similar to FIFO, so book value offers a good proxy for reproduction cost. The only adjustment here is for accounts receivable, which includes the allowance for doubtful accounts. The main adjustments in my NAV calculation concern non-current asset categories.

To make this simple, net PP&E, land use rights, and operating lease right-of-use assets are less than book value due to low inflation in China (1.6% 11-Y Avg), large decreases in leasing costs (warehouse, office, store, and delivery center), and understated depreciation and amortization.

The biggest adjustment is for intangible assets. The most relevant assets are the customer relationships or book of business, the labor force of 900,000 employees, and the product portfolio (which includes services). Below is a brief explanation of my calculations.

  • JD, which itself acts as a sales agency to third-party merchants, charges a technical service fee of around 5% to 8% and a distribution fee between 2% and 10%. It also charges a platform fee (which is fixed) and a security deposit (also fixed and dependent on the category of the product). Therefore, assuming a sales agency cost of 6.25% (average) and rounding up to 7% to account for any other extra costs, we can calculate the book base of JD's customers to be 7% times sustainable revenues of ~$160B, which equals ~$11.2B.
  • The average life of JD's software is around 4 years. Current R&D spending is $2.3B. Thus, the reproduction cost of its product (service) portfolio is 4 times $2.3B, which equals $9.3B.
  • The labor force, even though it is unionized, is not like the unions in the US, where the union negotiates wages with the companies; instead, JD has full control over its workers' wages. I took the weighted average salary at JD times the recruiting agency cost (~30% of the workers' salaries) times the current number of employees.

Total intangible assets are $27B, or roughly 2 years' worth of current SG&A (as of 2025, $13.7B in SG&A times 2 equals $27.4B).

Lastly, current liabilities and liabilities remain the same. The only adjustments are made for deferred tax assets and liabilities, which are discounted by 50% (normal adjustment for average businesses), since we don't know if JD will be able to fully capitalize on those future tax savings.

After all these adjustments, NAV in equity terms is around $63.2B. If we add total debt minus cash and non-core assets (cash, restricted cash, short-term investments, investments in equity investees, marketable securities and other investments, and deferred tax assets), the NAV in enterprise value terms is ~$30.7B.

Earnings Power Value

The first step is to find the current sustainable level of revenues, operating margins, and profits, as well as the sustainable corporate tax rate, in order to calculate a sustainable NOPAT.

As we can see, the current level of sustainable NOPAT is ~$2.2B. But we must adjust for excess or deficits in accounting D&A and find the actual cost of maintenance capex. Below is my estimate.

To make this simple, the model is saying that JD has understated D&A by around $116M on an average year between 2016 and 2025. But the current JD is about 60% bigger than the JD in an average year between 2016 and 2025. Thus, $116M increased to $176M. This amount is deducted from NOPAT to obtain an earnings power of $1.9B.

Lastly, we calculate the cost of capital and total debt.

After that, we can compute EPV on both bases, and we can see that JD is worth around $20.4B in EV terms and $52.9B in equity terms.

NAV & EPV vs The Market

The graph below shows what I wanted to prove in my thesis. JD doesn't produce enough earnings to cover the reproduction cost of its assets, and thus it doesn't produce excess returns. This alone proves there is no economic moat (as companies with moats have EPVs much higher than the reproduction cost of their assets), and JD is solely a business with somewhat competent management that operates in a highly competitive industry.

However, if we triangulate both estimates of value, we can compute an intrinsic value for JD based on current fundamentals.

As we can see, the estimated intrinsic value of JD is around $58B in equity terms. At the current market cap of $40B, there is a margin of safety of ~31% and even much higher in EV terms.

Risks

The risks to my thesis are very simple. As we have seen throughout this report, most of the value of JD is found in the value of its intangible assets, cash, restricted cash, and non-core assets that include various equity investments, wealth management products, loan receivables, and cash saved in money market funds. The thesis hinges on the fact that JD's assets and even the company's earnings power are underappreciated by the market, and just by the sheer fundamental value of JD, the market should eventually correct this current mispricing. However, as we know, this is a Chinese company, and the market tends to discount Chinese companies due to political instability and government intervention.

Another problem is management. So far, management has been able to increase revenue growth, but margins have dwindled a lot, and that has the market worried. The company is investing a lot of capital in investments and business projects that are more likely than not to produce earnings below the company's cost of capital and thus cause value destruction for shareholders. If operating margins were to fall further, or if the company were to have a net loss in the following years, that could crash the EPV of the company, causing further decline.

Also, this being a value investment might never materialize, as we know, it can take a long period of time for the market to realize the current value of JD and close this current discount gap.

Recommendation

Due to all the reasons above, I rate JD.com a buy. After my NAV and EPV analysis, JD should be worth around $58B, or $42.36 per share. What I found interesting is that JD's P/E Non-GAAP (FWD) is 10.06, while the sector median is 14.59. This implies that at the current price, the EPS (FWD) is ~$2.91, and if we apply the sector median multiple of 14.59, that yields a price of ~$42.46 per share, which is eerily close to my estimate. So, this investment reflects the expectation that JD will revert to the median and that operating margins and growth will normalize.

However, caution is still warranted due to all the risks mentioned above, and investors who are persuaded to make an investment in JD should avoid allocating a large portion of their portfolios to JD, since this is clearly not a compounder worth holding for 10, 20, or 30 years. Nonetheless, there is still potential to make a 47.7% return on investment.

data.title: HubSpot: Believe The Transition, But Wait For Confirmation
data.description:
HubSpot (HUBS) has undergone a significant reset over the last year, with the stock down more than 60%, putting its valuation closer in line with peers. The market is starting to price the company as a stable, mid-growth software provider instead of a premium SaaS name. However, it’s growing both revenues and customer count steadily and is moving upmarket. At the same time, HubSpot is investing in AI through tools like Breeze Assistant and a growing suite of agents. Depending on execution, AI-related growth could fuel upside. However, the current valuation creates some tension.

Expansion Remains Intact: Upmarket and Multi-Hub

The company’s Q4 and full-year 2025 report showed solid numbers, beating on both the top and the bottom line. For the full year, revenue hit approximately $3.1 billion, representing an 18% increase in constant currency, adding 40,000 net new customers. Net revenue retention (NRR) was at 103.5% for the year and accelerated to 105% in Q4, indicating improvement, with management guiding to another 1-2 point increase for 2026.

Meanwhile, net new ARR has exceeded revenue growth for six quarters in a row, which indicates stronger underlying demand. Upmarket momentum remains strong, with deals above $5,000 monthly recurring revenue up 33% and above $10,000 monthly recurring revenue up 41%. The quality mix is improving, as customers with over 500 seats increased five-fold over the year.

Multi-hub adoption continues to scale, with 62% of new Pro+ customers purchasing multiple hubs at entry and 40% of the installed base using four hubs or more by year-end. The average subscription revenue per customer of $11,700 showed modest growth. Overall expansion was driven primarily by cross-selling into additional hubs, seat growth, and deeper integration into customer workflows.

AI and Ecosystem Changes Are Reshaping the Model

Instead of being offered as standalone features, HubSpot’s AI strategy is now being directly embedded into the product through tools like Breeze Assistant. The AI agents are seeing increasing adoption, with the Customer Agent used by more than 8,000 customers, the Prospecting Agent by more than 10,000 customers, and the Data Agent by more than 2,500 customers. Customer Agent in particular accounted for roughly 60% of total credit consumption. This means AI is increasingly being used for core workflows like support ticket resolution, sales prospecting and outreach, and customer data enrichment.

Recently, the company announced a fresh $40.35 million investment in an AI-native R&D program in Ireland. Although this is not particularly significant compared to the overall TTM R&D spend of $906 million, according to Seeking Alpha figures, it does signal management’s commitment to embedding AI in core systems instead of just bolting on some features on top. According to management, this investment will support the company’s ambition of reaching half a million customers by 2028, up from its current count of 288,000 customers over 135 countries.

Valuation Versus Profitability: A Mixed Bag

Because the company mostly lost money between 2021 and 2024, trailing earnings valuations look lopsided. For example, the TTM P/E of 285x and the EV/EBITDA of 373x look extremely inflated. Both R&D and SG&A have been ramping up hard in recent years, with R&D going from $302 million in 2021 to $906 million in 2025, compressing margins and distorting earnings. However, the company finally printed a profit in fiscal 2025, and forward EPS estimates are very good, so let’s take it from there.

P/E Non-GAAP (FY1) of 19.7x is higher than Salesforce’s (CRM) 14x, but this is backed up by considerably higher revenue growth, with HubSpot’s forward revenue growth of 17.7% looking favorable compared to Salesforce’s 10%. HubSpot also looks a lot better on forward earnings expectations, with a 3-year EBITDA CAGR of 24.2%, handily beating out Salesforce’s 10.5%.

Profitability is where things get a bit more nuanced. HubSpot is currently still printing a flat EBITDA margin, while Salesforce is very strong at 30%. However, HubSpot’s gross margin of nearly 84% reflects genuine underlying strength, especially compared to Salesforce’s 77.7%. Essentially, at current prices, investors are paying for a net income margin and EBITDA margin that is not yet showing up in the numbers.

What Would Justify a Clear Buy

First of all, the operating margin needs to climb from its current level of around 0%, where a figure closer to 15-20% would bring it more in line with peers. Combined with the FCF margin staying around 20% or even expanding, this would support real conviction in terms of profitability and earnings quality.

Secondly, net revenue retention moving closer to 107-110% would signal stronger expansion within the existing customer base. Management’s hopes of a 500,000 customer count being realized would be a real catalyst, but even better monetization of current levels would make the transition story more credible.

Thirdly, clear signs of AI monetization need to emerge. A good reflection of this would be increased usage of Breeze agents translating into higher customer spend, showing up in the numbers as credit-based monetization improvements. More large customers adopting multiple hubs and an increase in the number of deals above $5,000 and $10,000 in monthly revenue would support management’s confident outlook. Even if SG&A and R&D spending stay at current levels, the aforementioned factors would give margins the boost they need to sustain real profitability.

If these factors begin to show up in Q1 and Q2 2026, particularly margin expansion and AI monetization, the stock would warrant a Buy rating from my side.

What Could Go Wrong

Concerns about factors like the slowdown in revenue growth and weak bottom line profitability have led to a plunge in valuation over the last few quarters. However, it's important to note that this is not entirely specific to HubSpot; Salesforce is also down 35% over the last year, indicating the SaaS sector as a whole is under pressure.

The most important issue for HubSpot is revenue growth. If it slows down further, for instance towards 10-15%, multiples could compress further. At the same time, there are persistent concerns regarding profitability. Although the gross margin remains strong, operating margins only recently broke even, and if SG&A and R&D expenses remain elevated, EPS estimates may be pressured and forward P/E ratios would drift higher. If HubSpot continues to lag Salesforce in profitability, investors may choose Salesforce as their exposure to the sector.

HubSpot is trying to do a lot of things at the same time: move upmarket, expand margins, and embed AI in core product offerings. Each of these initiatives alone introduces execution risk, but the combination is even more daunting. If any one of these fails to materialize in earnings, there is room for further downside.

Conclusion

HubSpot is a strong business with a sticky business model and ongoing upmarket momentum. Multi-hub adoption, improving retention, and increasing demand from larger customers are all contributing to the recent move from loss to roughly break-even. Indeed, margins are starting to move from negative to positive. However, at current valuations, much of this improvement is already priced in, particularly on earnings-based metrics. The company is making real strides towards AI monetization, but until this shows up in the next few quarterly reports, I rate the stock a Hold.

data.title: Graphic Packaging Holding: Falling Earnings Make This A Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Graphic Packaging Holding Company (GPK) currently trades at $9.23, which looks cheap since it is down over 60% in the past one year. It has a ~4.7% dividend yield, and that, plus the 6-9x multiples, makes it look undervalued.

That is, however, an incomplete view of the situation. GPK’s business has weakened significantly, with revenue down and EPS declining 30% YoY. Topline performance was still good last year, but margins are under pressure. That shows in their recent quarterly EPS miss despite a revenue beat.

Management has therefore taken proactive measures, going into a consolidation mode instead of growth. They now want to control costs, normalize inventory, and repair their balance sheet. Their 2026 guided EBITDA is now much less than 2025 numbers, and free cash flow is guided to ~$700–800 million. These numbers are now coming mostly due to lower capex and cost control, not from any major improvement in the business.

The reason for GPK’s price decline is now clear. The core reason: growth has stalled, considerable capex has been spent, but repair has not happened. Just because valuation and yield look attractive on the surface does not mean rerating will occur without proper execution. On top of other difficulties is the huge $5.9 billion debt on a $2.76 billion equity base.

A strong rerating will only happen if all these things are done properly - margins are restored, earnings stabilize, and leverage goes down. Until that becomes visible, the stock is likely to remain constrained despite its low valuation. As such, I rate GPK a Hold.

Business Overview

Graphic Packaging is a global paperboard packaging company working in the consumer staples end markets. Its products include fiber-based packaging used in food, household, and health products. It does business in 26 countries with 3000 customers.

GPK’s revenue base is diversified but still concentrated in consumer-facing categories: ~38% from food, ~25% from foodservice, ~21% from beverages, ~12% from household products, and ~4% from health & beauty. These are relatively stable end markets, although not immune from cyclical pressures. The pressure shows up in changing volume demands tied to spending trends, inventory cycles, and customer ordering patterns.

GPK is, at its core, a capital-intensive, margin-sensitive business. Its revenue drivers are consumer demand and orders, driving the volume, and contracts and pass-through mechanisms, driving pricing. It needs paperboard and pulp as raw materials, it needs energy and transportation, and it has manufacturing overhead. These things determine its cost structure.
