GPK’s margins depend on its ability to pass through incremental costs, utilize capacity optimally, and operational efficiency. These things make the business sensitive to demand swings and margin cycles, which is precisely what we are seeing now.

Over the last few years, GPK went through a multi-billion-dollar capex expansion targeting capacity and facility modernization. The idea was to maintain its position as a leader in sustainable packaging. That program is now largely complete, and management is guiding to lower capex and higher FCF.

Financials

GPK had $8.8 billion in revenue in 2024. Adjusted EBITDA was ~$1.7 billion. In 2025, revenue fell 2% to $8.6 billion, but adjusted EBITDA dropped 20% to ~$1.4 billion. Revenue holding mostly steady here means demand is steady, but the sharp drop in EBITDA implies margin pressure. That is guided to drop even further to ~$1.05–1.25 billion in 2026, which is another ~20% decline. This is a direct outcome of the 600 bps decline in margins and means that the business is generating much less profit per dollar of sales, even though sales haven’t fallen much.

Other metrics also show the same deterioration. Revenue growth has fallen 2.16% YoY, EPS diluted growth is -31.48% YoY, gross margin is 18.72%, EBIT margin is 9.91%, and net income margin is 5.15%.

However, GPK’s core problem today is that this capex expansion has not resulted in immediate benefits. Demand has remained steady, but margins have decreased. They now have a very high leverage of ~$5.9 billion, with a debt/EBITDA ratio of nearly ~5.5x - this is too high for this type of business. Basically, their expansion happened at an inopportune time, just when demand softness and margin pressure hit them.

The balance sheet improvement they are expecting is not coming from higher earnings as a result of the expansion effort. It is coming from lower expenses as a result of completing the expansion effort. That is a net-negative story, where FCF is increasing because expenses that were used to transition the company are falling, not because the transition is having meaningful improvement in the business. And the end result for investors is that the stock price is going down continuously.

Dividend

Its dividend remains one of the few clear positive angles for GPK. Its forward dividend yield is about 4.71%, its annual payout is $0.44 per share, and its payout ratio is about 24.44%. Seeking Alpha rates its dividend safety grade a B- and dividend yield grade an A+ relative to the sector.

The dividend does look well-covered on the surface. That payout ratio is not the profile of a company under immediate dividend pressure. However, it isn’t the present safety that’s the concern, but future growth and quality. Noteworthy is that the company has 1 year of consecutive dividend growth and 10 years of consecutive dividend payments, both below sector norms.

The market has re-rated the yield not because the annual payout in dollar value has increased, but because the share price has fallen. In July 2025, forward yield was shown as 1.97%. By late March 2026, it rose to 4.71%—not because the dividend suddenly became high, but because the stock price collapsed.

So the right way to frame GPK’s dividend is that it is currently safe enough, but it lacks the consistency required of a high-quality income stream. Hence, it does not carry the whole investment case by itself.

Valuation

GPK’s valuation needs to be looked at on a forward basis because trailing multiples appear cheaper due to higher historical earnings. Now, GPK’s enterprise value is around $8.34 billion. The midpoint of 2026 EBITDA guidance is $1.15 billion. The current EV/EBITDA multiple is 6.04x. Forward is 7.5, which is slightly below sector. So the stock is not distressed. It is trading at discounted, not broken multiples.

Scenario Analysis

Scenario

At current prices ($9.23), GPK is effectively priced between the bear and the base case, implying that the market is assuming stabilization, not recovery. If margins improve and earnings stabilize, we will get to the base case, which offers around 30% upside.

The bull case shows that upside could be substantial if GPK executes properly. But that will need cost control as well as sustained improvement in earnings. On the other hand, the bear case shows the impact of high leverage, so even a modest decline in EBITDA causes a large decline in equity value. That tells me that the risk-reward profile is asymmetric.

Another interesting point is how sensitive equity value is to EBITDA. At current multiples, a $200-300 million change in EBITDA means a ~$1.5–2.5 billion change in enterprise value. Debt is fixed at ~$5.9 billion, so that entire change goes towards improving equity value. So marginal improvement in operating performance vastly improves the stock’s performance.

Risks to the Hold Thesis

The biggest risk to a Hold rating is that execution improves faster than expected. If GPK can recover even a part of the earlier EBITDA, then, like I noted, because of leverage, incremental EBITDA disproportionately benefits equity. So, a Hold in such a scenario would be too conservative.

The second risk is that if FCF improves in 2026, that could re-rate the stock. Currently, FCF yield is ~30%. Even if a part of this is cyclical, it may improve. If FCF improves, that may cause a reduction in debt. If there’s even slight deleveraging, equity value will again improve significantly.

Any decent deleveraging will change the narrative. Assume that GPK is able to achieve a target ~$500 million reduction. That could make leverage move from 5x to 4x. This will immediately reduce risk perception and expand equity multiples. That means, even if there’s no strong growth, just balance sheet improvement could drive upside.

Consumer markets are currently defensive, but they could stabilize faster than expected. A majority of GPK’s revenue comes from food, beverages, and household goods. If volume stabilizes here and GPK ends inventory destocking, then margins will recover positively via utilization. So earnings recovery may happen simply from normalization, not growth.

Currently, GPK’s peers trade in the 8-10x EV/EBITDA range, while GPK trades a little lower than that. If GPK demonstrates some stability and reduces volatility, then simply from sentiment improvement, multiples can expand even without major earnings growth.

Conclusion

GPK is going through significant changes—most of them negative. All its key numbers have fallen. Even so, the market may find its current low prices and strong dividend attractive.

However, the valuation merely reflects weak earnings. It has no downside protection at all if execution does not occur properly. Therefore, I rate GPK a Hold.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Index Inches Closer To Correction Territory
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 finished the week at its lowest level in over seven months and is now inches away from correction territory, sitting 8.74% off its all-time high from January 27, 2026. The index posted its fifth consecutive weekly loss, its longest streak since 2022, falling 2.1% from last Friday. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been below the 50-day moving average since February 27th, 2026, and below the 200-day moving average since March 19th, 2026. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st, 2025.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, 2025, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 1.33%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently down 6.96% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is down 1.56% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).
